sections.heading: 0. Frame
sections.body: Subject: the Tracking contract (scope.md), i.e. persistence of workstream/PM state - objective, iterations, plan, decisions, retro, discovery. `Repository` is its v1 local-filesystem implementation. Goal of discovery: map the contract surface, find where the engine reaches around it to touch the filesystem (the real obstacle to a second backing), compare second-backing options, sketch how a backing is selected, and capture the rename question. No engine code beyond the one-line stale-skeleton fix (DoD #7), done.

sections.heading: 1. Contract surface today (Repository)
sections.body:
Public methods on lume/repository.py:
- find_lume_dir() -> Path|None : walk start + parents for a `.lume/` dir.
- workstreams(lume_dir) -> [Workstream] : list, sorted by slug.
- active_workstreams(lume_dir) -> [Workstream] : filter not-closed.
- workstream(slug=None) -> Workstream : resolve by slug, or sole-active default; named errors for unknown/closed/zero/multiple.
- reopen_workstream(slug) -> Workstream : flip closed -> active.
- load_state(slug) -> dict : read+validate state.json.
- save_state(slug, doc) : validate+write state.json.
- create_workstream(slug, title) -> Workstream : make dir + initial state.json + objective.json.
Private: _require_lume_dir, _workstream_dirs (glob dirs containing state.json), _load_workstream, _state_path.

Filesystem assumptions baked in: a `.lume/` directory discoverable by walking parents; `workstreams/<slug>/` directories; one `state.json` per dir; slug == directory name; per-file pathlib read/write; existence checks via Path.is_file/exists; `mkdir` to create. Identity (`Workstream.name`) is derived from the directory name.

sections.heading: 2. Contract-leak audit (the load-bearing finding)
sections.body:
The Tracking contract is SPLIT and mislocated. Repository owns `.lume/` discovery + state.json by slug, but `Workstream` holds its own `self._path: Path` and does direct filesystem I/O for everything else:
- workstream.py _load_objective/_save_objective: read/write objective.json (json.loads / write_text).
- workstream.py _save_iter_content: write iterations/NNN.json; open_iteration does iterations_dir.mkdir.
- workstream.py add_decision/_save_decisions: read/write decisions.json.
- workstream.py save_retro: write retro.json.
- workstream.py _save_state: writes state.json DIRECTLY via `state.save(self._path / STATE_FILE, ...)` - NOT via Repository.save_state. So even state persistence bypasses the seam on the write path.

Consequence: a second backing implemented as a Repository subclass would intercept discovery + create + load_state, but NONE of the artifact writes (objective/iteration/decisions/retro) nor Workstream's own state save. The contract boundary is in the wrong place - it must sit below Workstream, not beside it.

Other sites:
- cli.py retro verb reaches into `ws._path / 'retro.json'` and reads it directly (lines ~338-341) - a leak through the model's private path.
- cli.py prints filesystem paths (ws.objective_path, ws.iterations_dir) in user messages - cosmetic, but assumes a path exists; a non-fs backing has none.
- Workstream exposes Path properties (iterations_dir, objective_path) and derives `name` from self._path.name - path-coupled identity.
- state.py load/save take a Path and do pure json + validation; this is the serialization+validation layer the contract would wrap (not itself a leak).
- migrate.py: ~10 read/write sites (legacy .md reads + JSON writes via Workstream helpers + direct state save). Inherently local-files-and-markdown; treat as a one-shot LOCAL-ONLY bridge, not part of the live contract (it cannot and need not run against a remote backing).

sections.heading: 3. Second-backing options
sections.body:
(a) Real service - GitHub Issues (scope.md's named pair). Map workstream -> issue/project, iterations/plan/decisions/retro -> issue body/comments or sub-issues. Proves replaceability strongly and is the real target, but heavy: auth, network, rate limits, mapping rich validated JSON onto issue fields, and test-ability needs HTTP mocking/VCR. High cost before the contract boundary is even clean.

(b) In-process proof - either an in-memory store (dict keyed by slug) or a second on-disk format (e.g. a single SQLite file, or one JSON blob per workstream in a different layout). Cheap, fully deterministic in tests, no external deps. SQLite is the strongest proof here: a genuinely non-file storage model (rows, one connection) that breaks EVERY path assumption and forces the boundary to the right place. In-memory is the cheapest and is ideal as a test double.

Recommendation: PROOF-FIRST. Build an in-process backing (in-memory for tests + a SQLite or single-blob store as the real second implementation), defer GitHub. Rationale: scope.md's design test only asks to express the second member of the pair cleanly; a non-filesystem store is the cheapest thing that actually exercises the contract and exposes the leaks in section 2. constraints.md: 'don't fully abstract until a second implementation pulls on it' + 'cheapest insurance against single-implementation contracts that leak.' GitHub becomes a follow-on once the boundary is clean.

sections.heading: 4. Selection contract (sketch)
sections.body: Backing chosen by CONSTRUCTOR INJECTION, decided once at the edge: cli.main constructs the concrete store and injects it; no engine code below branches on backing type. An env var (e.g. LUME_BACKING=fs|sqlite|memory) read once in cli.main selects which concrete store to build; default = fs, so existing behaviour is unchanged. The store is passed into Repository (or Repository becomes the store interface and the concrete class is the implementation). Tests inject the in-memory store directly, no env needed. Keeps the 'config over code' + 'design for replaceability' constraints.

sections.heading: 5. Naming fork (Repository -> Tracking contract)
sections.body: `Repository` is the implementation name, overloaded (git repo / DDD repository) in a tool living inside a git repo. The contract is scope.md's Tracking contract; siblings are Review and Practice (and future: inference/config, artifacts, telemetry). KEY entanglement discovered in section 2: the contract is bigger than today's Repository - to be swappable it must also own the artifact + state writes Workstream currently does itself. So renaming is not a sed; it travels with a boundary refactor (move persistence out of Workstream behind the injected store). Candidate names: Tracker, TrackingStore, WorkstreamStore, StateStore. DECISION DEFERRED to planning; flagged that rename ~= refactor.

sections.heading: 6. Open forks for planning
sections.body:
F1. Real (GitHub) vs proof (SQLite/in-memory) backing for v2 - discovery recommends proof-first.
F2. Where the contract boundary goes: does Workstream lose self._path and route ALL persistence (objective/iteration/decisions/retro/state) through an injected store? How much of this refactor lands in v1 vs later? (This is the load-bearing decision.)
F3. Contract shape: per-artifact get/put(slug, artifact, doc) vs whole-workstream load/save(slug, doc). A non-file store (SQLite/GitHub) may prefer one blob per workstream; the filesystem split is incidental.
F4. Rename Repository (F5 below) - and how to keep it distinct from Review/Practice contracts.
F5. Selection mechanism: env var vs config file vs constructor-only.
F6. migrate.py: confirm it stays a local-only one-shot bridge, excluded from the live contract.
F7. cli.py path-printing + the retro verb's direct ws._path read: route through the model/store so output doesn't assume a filesystem path.

sections.heading: 7. Scope held / deferred
sections.body: No engine code changed this iteration except the one permitted one-line fix: iteration.py _SKELETONS discovery prompt now says 'discovery.json' instead of 'discovery.md' (leftover from the JSON-only flip). Suite stays green. Everything else (the boundary refactor, the second backing, the rename, the selection wiring) is deferred to planning/execution and captured as forks above. Artifact is this discovery.json only - no discovery.md.
